reason_text: Clear food hardship should move SNAP higher in the recommendation order.
Possible food hardship supports recommending SNAP, but with slightly less urgency than a clear signal.
Recent income disruption can increase urgency for food assistance.
Households with children should be prioritized somewhat higher for food assistance support.
Older-adult or disability-related needs can raise the practical importance of SNAP even when eligibility needs closer review.
No health coverage is a strong signal to prioritize Medicaid screening.
Underinsurance still indicates unmet healthcare risk and can justify a Medicaid recommendation.
Pregnancy should elevate Medicaid because coverage can begin during pregnancy and continue postpartum.
Children in the household increase the value of screening for Medicaid/MA child coverage.
An elderly or disabled household member raises the value of Medicaid review even when Phase 2 rules treat the case as partially ambiguous.
A household that needs help with heating bills should prioritize LIHEAP.
High utility burden is a strong signal for LIHEAP urgency and possible crisis follow-up.
Recent job loss can make heating costs newly unaffordable and increases LIHEAP urgency.
Households with elderly or disabled members may face higher risk from heating instability.
Households with children may warrant slightly higher LIHEAP priority because heating instability affects the entire family.
